gold_final_answer: (a, b, c) with a | b and b | c
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt: Planes through the points with integer coordinates in the three dimensional Euclidean space partition the space into unit cubes. Find all triples $(a, b, c)$, $a \le b \le c$, of positive integers such that the cubes can be colored in $abc$ colors in such a way that every parallelepiped of dimensions $a \times b \times c$, integer vertices and faces parallel to the coordinate planes does not contain cubes of the same color.
response_text:
Let the cubes be the unit cubes of the standard lattice in \(\mathbb Z^3\), and suppose we color all cubes with \(abc\) colors so that in every axis-parallel \(a\times b\times c\) box with integer vertices, all \(abc\) cubes have distinct colors.

We must determine all triples
\[
(a,b,c),\qquad a\le b\le c,
\]
for which this is possible.

---

## 1. Reformulation

Identify each unit cube with its “lower-left-front” corner \((x,y,z)\in \mathbb Z^3\).  
Then a coloring is a map
\[
f:\mathbb Z^3\to \{1,2,\dots,abc\}.
\]
The condition says:

> For every box
> \[
> \{x,\dots,x+a-1\}\times \{y,\dots,y+b-1\}\times \{z,\dots,z+c-1\},
> \]
> the \(abc\) values of \(f\) on these points are all distinct.

Since there are exactly \(abc\) cubes in such a box and exactly \(abc\) colors, this means:

> Every such \(a\times b\times c\) box contains each color exactly once.

We will prove this is possible **iff**
\[
a,b,c \text{ are pairwise coprime.}
\]

---

## 2. Necessity: pairwise coprimeness is required

We first show that
\[
\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(a,c)=\gcd(b,c)=1
\]
is necessary.

### Step 1: periodicity in each coordinate

Fix \(y,z\). Consider the line of cubes
\[
(x,y,z),\qquad x\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Take two consecutive boxes:
\[
[x,x+a-1]\times [y,y+b-1]\times [z,z+c-1]
\]
and
\[
[x+1,x+a]\times [y,y+b-1]\times [z,z+c-1].
\]
Each contains every color exactly once. Their overlap contains all cubes except the two “slabs”
\[
\{x\}\times [y,y+b-1]\times [z,z+c-1]
\]
and
\[
\{x+a\}\times [y,y+b-1]\times [z,z+c-1].
\]
Therefore these two slabs must have exactly the same multiset of colors. Since this holds for every \(y,z\), repeating the same argument in the \(y\)- and \(z\)-directions gives:

- shifting by \(a\) in the \(x\)-direction preserves the color multiset on every \(b\times c\) slab;
- similarly for \(b\) and \(c\).

A standard one-cube version of this argument yields in fact
\[
f(x+a,y,z)=f(x,y,z),\qquad
f(x,y+b,z)=f(x,y,z),\qquad
f(x,y,z+c)=f(x,y,z).
\]
So the coloring is periodic modulo \((a,b,c)\).

Thus it is determined by the \(abc\) cubes in
\[
R=[0,a-1]\times[0,b-1]\times[0,c-1],
\]
and these \(abc\) cubes must all have different colors.

So we may think of the color as a label of the residue class
\[
(x\bmod a,\ y\bmod b,\ z\bmod c).
\]

### Step 2: what happens if \(\gcd(a,b)>1\)

Assume \(d=\gcd(a,b)>1\). Consider the translate of \(R\) by vector \((1,1,0)\):
\[
R'=\{(x+1,y+1,z):(x,y,z)\in R\}.
\]
This is also an \(a\times b\times c\) box, so it also must contain each color exactly once.

But because colors are periodic mod \((a,b,c)\), the color of a cube depends only on \((x\bmod a,y\bmod b,z\bmod c)\). Hence the map
\[
(i,j,k)\mapsto (i+1 \bmod a,\ j+1 \bmod b,\ k)
\]
must permute the \(abc\) colors.

Now iterate this shift \( \operatorname{lcm}(a,b)/d\)-style: since \(\gcd(a,b)=d>1\), the orbit of \((i,j)\mapsto(i+1,j+1)\) on \(\mathbb Z_a\times\mathbb Z_b\) has length \(\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)<ab\). Therefore the pairs \((i,j)\) split into several orbits. In particular, some two different positions in an \(a\times b\) layer are forced to have the same color, contradicting that one box must contain all colors distinctly.

A cleaner way to see the contradiction: if two positions \((i,j)\) and \((i',j')\) satisfy
\[
i'-i\equiv t\pmod a,\qquad j'-j\equiv t\pmod b
\]
for some \(t\), then repeated use of the “shifted box has same color set” property forces equality of colors along such diagonals. Since \(d>1\), there are at least two distinct points in \([0,a-1]\times[0,b-1]\) with the same value of \(i-j\pmod d\), so two cubes in one \(a\times b\times c\) box get the same color — impossible.

Hence \(\gcd(a,b)=1\). By symmetry,
\[
\gcd(a,c)=\gcd(b,c)=1.
\]

So pairwise coprimeness is necessary.

---

## 3. Sufficiency: pairwise coprimeness allows such a coloring

Now assume
\[
\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(a,c)=\gcd(b,c)=1.
\]

We construct an explicit coloring.

Because the numbers are pairwise coprime, by the Chinese remainder theorem,
\[
\mathbb Z/(abc)\mathbb Z \cong \mathbb Z/a\mathbb Z \times \mathbb Z/b\mathbb Z \times \mathbb Z/c\mathbb Z.
\]
So we may encode the triple of residues \((x\bmod a,y\bmod b,z\bmod c)\) by a single residue mod \(abc\).

Define the color of cube \((x,y,z)\) to be the residue class
\[
f(x,y,z)\equiv x\pmod a,\qquad
f(x,y,z)\equiv y\pmod b,\qquad
f(x,y,z)\equiv z\pmod c,
\]
interpreted as one of \(abc\) colors via CRT.

Equivalently, two cubes have the same color iff
\[
x\equiv x' \pmod a,\quad y\equiv y' \pmod b,\quad z\equiv z' \pmod c.
\]

Now take any \(a\times b\times c\) box
\[
\{x_0,\dots,x_0+a-1\}\times\{y_0,\dots,y_0+b-1\}\times\{z_0,\dots,z_0+c-1\}.
\]
As \(x\) runs through \(a\) consecutive integers, the residues mod \(a\) are all distinct; similarly for \(y\) mod \(b\), \(z\) mod \(c\). Therefore the \(abc\) cubes in the box realize all triples
\[
(\alpha,\beta,\gamma)\in \mathbb Z_a\times\mathbb Z_b\times\mathbb Z_c
\]
exactly once, hence by CRT all \(abc\) colors exactly once.

So the condition is satisfied.

---

## 4. Conclusion

The coloring exists exactly for pairwise coprime \(a,b,c\).

\[
\boxed{\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(a,c)=\gcd(b,c)=1.}
\]

Final answer: \((a,b,c)\) are exactly the triples of positive integers with \(a\le b\le c\) such that \(a,b,c\) are pairwise coprime.

grade.predicted: \((a,b,c)\) are exactly the triples of positive integers with \(a\le b\le c\) such that \(a,b,c\) are pairwise coprime.
